senses.definition: A historical stage of Indo-Aryan languages, seen as a late form of Prakrit and a step toward modern North Indian languages.
examples: Scholars study Apabhramsa to understand the development of modern Indo-Aryan languages.
Many early Jain texts were written in Apabhramsa.
The poet used Apabhramsa forms alongside Sanskrit in his work.

senses.definition: Quickly or at a fast rate.
examples: Technological change is continuing apace.
The company is growing apace in international markets.
Construction on the new campus is moving apace.

senses.definition: A member of a group of Native American peoples originally living in the southwestern United States and northern Mexico.
examples: The museum hosted an exhibition on Apache history and culture.
She is researching the traditional crafts of the Apache peoples.
An Apache elder spoke about preserving their language.

senses.definition: In old French usage, a young criminal or street ruffian, especially in early 20th‑century Paris.
examples: Newspapers at the time warned people about violent Apaches in the streets.
The film portrays Parisian Apaches as dangerous gang members.
Writers used the word Apache to describe tough urban ruffians.

senses.definition: Plural of Apache, referring either to the Native American peoples or, in old French usage, to urban street ruffians.
examples: The Apaches resisted colonization for many years.
Writers of the period often mentioned Parisian Apaches in their stories.
Tourists learned about the Apaches during the guided tour.

senses.definition: An obsolete term for a female Apache, especially in the old French sense of a female street ruffian.
examples: The novel includes an apachette who leads a small gang.
Journalists sometimes used the term apachette for women in such circles.
The apachette in the story is portrayed as fearless and proud.

senses.definition: Criminal or violent behavior associated with urban gangs, especially in early 20th‑century Paris.
examples: Moralists at the time warned about the spread of apachism.
The judge condemned the rise of apachism in the city.
Newspaper articles linked apachism with poverty and neglect.

senses.definition: A person associated with Apache gangs or culture, especially in historical French use.
examples: The police arrested several apachites after the fight.
Writers described the apachites as symbols of urban decay.
He was considered an apachite because of his rough lifestyle.

senses.definition: In ancient Persian architecture, a large columned audience hall or palace, especially at Persepolis.
examples: The Apadana at Persepolis is famous for its grand reliefs.
Archaeologists studied the remains of the royal apadana.
Visitors can still see the tall columns of the Apadana palace.

senses.definition: Plural of apadana, referring to large columned audience halls in ancient Persian palaces.
examples: The ruins include several apadanas and smaller chambers.
Scholars compared the designs of different apadanas.
These apadanas were used for royal ceremonies.

senses.definition: Loss or absence of normal sensation in part or all of the body.
examples: The patient showed signs of apaesthesia in the lower limbs.
Severe nerve damage can lead to apaesthesia.
Doctors monitored the extent of apaesthesia after the surgery.

senses.definition: Relating to or marked by loss or absence of normal sensation.
examples: The surgeon noted apaesthetic areas around the wound.
Apaesthetic skin may be more easily injured.
An apaesthetic limb can hide signs of damage.

senses.definition: To make an area of the body lose normal sensation.
examples: The doctor used a local drug to apaesthetize the area.
They apaesthetized the skin before the minor operation.
The dentist will apaesthetize the tooth and surrounding gum.

senses.definition: In a way that causes or involves loss of normal sensation.
examples: The drug acts apaestically on nerve endings.
The limb responded apaestically to the treatment.
These agents work apaestically rather than causing pain.

senses.definition: An old exclamation meaning 'away!' or 'begone!', used to tell someone or something to go away.
examples: The character cries 'Apage!' to drive away the demons.
In some old texts, priests shout 'Apage, Satan!'
He jokingly said 'Apage!' when the cat climbed on the table.

senses.definition: A method of proving something by showing that the opposite leads to a contradiction.
examples: The philosopher used apagoge to complete his argument.
In mathematics, apagoge is common in proofs by contradiction.
His reasoning followed the pattern of apagoge: assume the opposite, then refute it.

senses.definition: Plural of apagoge, referring to proofs by showing the opposite leads to a contradiction.
examples: The textbook includes several apagoges to illustrate the method.
In advanced logic, students practice forming apagoges.
These apagoges help learners see why the claim must be true.

senses.definition: Using or relating to proof by showing that the opposite leads to a contradiction.
examples: He presented an apagogic argument to support his claim.
Apagogic reasoning is central in some branches of mathematics.
The paper explains the apagogic structure of the proof.

senses.definition: Relating to or involving argument that proves a statement by showing that its denial is impossible or absurd.
examples: The theorem is established through apagogical reasoning.
He gave a detailed apagogical demonstration.
Apagogical methods are often used to show impossibility results.

senses.definition: In a way that proves something by showing that its opposite leads to a contradiction.
examples: The proposition is apagogically established.
They argued apagogically rather than by direct construction.
Some classical results are best proved apagogically.

senses.definition: A type of argument that proves a claim by showing that the opposite is impossible or absurd.
examples: The proof is an apagogue based on a simple contradiction.
He used an apagogue to show that the assumption must be false.
This apagogue is a classic example from Greek philosophy.

senses.definition: Not paid or having received no payment.
examples: Several invoices remain apaid at the end of the month.
He complained about apaid overtime hours.
The accountant listed all apaid fees in a separate column.

senses.definition: An old spelling of 'impair', meaning to make something weaker or worse.
examples: Such habits will apair his health over time.
They feared that heavy taxes would apair trade.
Constant noise may apair concentration.

senses.definition: A rare form of 'appease', meaning to calm someone or make a problem less severe.
examples: He tried to apaise the angry crowd.
They offered a small gift to apaise their host.
Her gentle words helped apaise his fears.

senses.definition: A member of a Native American people who lived in what is now northern Florida.
examples: The Apalachee fought against Spanish colonization.
Archaeologists study Apalachee sites in the region.
Apalachee history is an important part of Florida's past.

senses.definition: A town in the province of Pampanga in the Philippines.
examples: Apalit is famous for its river festival.
They drove from Manila to Apalit for the weekend.
Researchers documented local traditions in Apalit.

senses.definition: An ancient female given name, especially known from wives and daughters of Hellenistic rulers of Persian origin.
examples: Apama was the name of several queens in the Seleucid dynasty.
The historian wrote a chapter on Apama and her influence at court.
Coins were minted bearing the name of Apama.

senses.definition: A right, benefit, or property that belongs only to a particular person or group.
examples: Education is no longer the apanage of the rich.
Some people once thought that high culture was the apanage of the elite.
Democracy should not be the apanage of a single region.

senses.definition: In history, land or income given to a younger child in a royal or noble family.
examples: The king granted an apanage to his younger son.
These lands formed the prince's apanage.
An apanage ensured that each royal child had resources.

senses.definition: Having been provided with an apanage, especially land or income granted by a royal family.
examples: The apanaged princes ruled their own territories.
Each apanaged branch of the family held some power.
Apanaged nobles depended on the king's favor.

senses.definition: Plural of apanage, meaning special rights, benefits, or lands given to certain people, especially in royal families.
examples: Different princes received different apanages.
The apanages helped prevent conflict among royal heirs.
His descendants enjoyed generous apanages.

senses.definition: Present participle of 'apanage', meaning giving land, income, or rights to someone, especially in a royal family.
examples: The king was apanaging his younger sons with distant territories.
By apanaging his relatives, he tried to keep the peace.
Apanaging princes created semi‑independent states.

senses.definition: Absence or loss of the testicles.
examples: Apandry may result from a congenital condition.
The doctor diagnosed apandry after a careful examination.
Cases of apandry are discussed in medical literature.

senses.definition: A genus of small parasitic wasps whose larvae develop inside other insects.
examples: Apanteles species are used in biological control programs.
Researchers observed Apanteles attacking caterpillars.
The farmer released Apanteles to reduce the pest population.

senses.definition: An old genus name for certain tiger moths, now usually placed in another genus.
examples: Several species once placed in Apantesis are now reclassified.
The collector still uses the name Apantesis on his labels.
Apantesis appears in older field guides to moths.

senses.definition: A strong dislike of being with people; a tendency to avoid human company.
examples: His apanthropia made social gatherings very stressful.
She struggled with apanthropia after years of isolation.
Apanthropia can be mistaken for simple shyness.

senses.definition: A dislike of human society and a wish to live apart from other people.
examples: The character's apanthropy leads him to live in the mountains.
She wrote about her apanthropy and desire for solitude.
Apanthropy may grow after repeated negative experiences.

senses.definition: A rare spelling variant of 'aper', meaning a person who imitates others.
examples: He dismissed the painter as an apar with little originality.
Critics saw her as an apar of more famous writers.
An apar copies style but not the deeper meaning.

senses.definition: A small indigenous group living in parts of northern South America, especially Brazil and French Guiana.
examples: The Aparai maintain many traditional practices.
Anthropologists have worked with the Aparai for decades.
The Aparai language is considered endangered.

senses.definition: Lacking an epiphysis or similar bony growth at the end of a bone.
examples: The species has an aparaphysate vertebral structure.
Some fish show aparaphysate bones in the tail.
An aparaphysate condition can affect movement.

senses.definition: In Indian philosophy, lower or worldly knowledge, especially knowledge of objects rather than spiritual truth.
examples: The text distinguishes between aparavidya and higher, spiritual knowledge.
Science is sometimes classed as aparavidya.
He warned against focusing only on aparavidya and forgetting inner growth.

senses.definition: An old or dialect spelling of 'pardon', meaning to forgive someone or officially say that they are not guilty.
examples: The king chose to apardon the rebels.
They begged him to apardon their mistakes.
She refused to apardon the offense.

senses.definition: A type of pack‑saddle and its equipment used especially in Spanish‑American regions for carrying loads on animals.
examples: The mule's aparejo was carefully adjusted before the journey.
He loaded the bags evenly on the aparejo.
Traditional ranchers still use an aparejo in the mountains.

senses.definition: Plural of aparejo, referring to pack‑saddles and their equipment used for carrying loads on animals.
examples: The stable stored several old aparejos.
They checked the aparejos before starting the trip.
Workers loaded the mules' aparejos with supplies.

senses.definition: Extreme sexual coldness or lack of sexual desire.
examples: The case report described symptoms of apargia.
Apargia can have psychological and physical causes.
The therapist addressed her concerns about apargia carefully.

senses.definition: Counting or numbering of things one by one, often in a rhetorical list.
examples: The speech used aparithmesis to list every injustice.
Through aparithmesis, he emphasized each point in turn.
Writers sometimes use aparithmesis for dramatic effect.

senses.definition: With a distance or space between things or people.
examples: The two houses are about 50 meters apart.
Please sit a little further apart.
The chairs were placed apart to make room.

senses.definition: Separate from something else or considered separately.
examples: Apart from the price, I like this car.
The issue should be discussed apart from personal feelings.
Leave this question apart for the moment.

senses.definition: Into pieces.
examples: The old book fell apart in my hands.
The toy broke apart easily.
If you pull too hard, it will come apart.

senses.definition: Separate or different from other things or people.
examples: They live in apart rooms now.
He led an apart life, away from the city.
Their apart paths rarely crossed.

senses.definition: In some Spanish‑speaking countries, a post office box or numbered postal address.
examples: Please send your application to this apartado.
The letter was addressed to Apartado 123.
They rented an apartado for business mail.

senses.definition: The former official system in South Africa that separated people by race and gave white people more rights.
examples: Apartheid in South Africa ended in the early 1990s.
Many people around the world protested against apartheid.
He wrote a book about life under apartheid.

senses.definition: Any system that separates people or groups in an unfair and strict way, similar to the former South African system.
examples: Critics called the policy a form of educational apartheid.
Some describe the situation as economic apartheid.
Gender apartheid limits women's freedom in some places.

senses.definition: Plural of apartheid, used to refer to different systems or situations of strict and unfair separation.
examples: Writers have spoken about various modern apartheids.
They campaigned against social and economic apartheids.
Different apartheids can exist in the same society.

senses.definition: A hotel that has apartments where guests can cook their own meals as well as ordinary hotel rooms.
examples: We stayed in an aparthotel near the city center.
An aparthotel is convenient for long business trips.
They booked an aparthotel so they could prepare their own food.

senses.definition: Plural of aparthotel, meaning hotels with apartment‑style rooms and cooking facilities.
examples: Aparthotels are popular with families.
Many cities now offer several aparthotels.
They compared prices of different aparthotels online.
